title: Inflation and stagflation in Oxford Economics’ IFRS 9 and CECL Scenarios
gist: Oxford Economics explains why its statistical, distribution-driven IFRS 9/CECL downside scenarios show inflation falling (not spiking) even amid the US/Israel-Iran war, arguing this demand-driven approach is structurally more robust and often more severe on credit-loss drivers than narrative-based stagflation scenarios.

themes.theme: Global inflation shock from Iran war

insights.claim: Global CPI inflation is projected to peak at over 4.5% in 2026 as a result of the US/Israel-Iran war, compared to an almost 9% peak in 2022 driven by the Russian invasion of Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: A tangible, quantified inflation-shock benchmark for 2026 that investors can compare against realized CPI prints and central bank reaction functions.
insights.quote: Globally, CPI inflation reached almost 9% in 2022 due to the Russian invasion of Ukraine and is projected to peak at over 4.5% in 2026 as a result of the US/Israel-Iran war.
insights.locator: Conflict-related commodity price shocks and supply chain disruptions

insights.claim: Energy price spikes from both the 2022 Russia-Ukraine war and the 2026 US/Israel-Iran war reduced real disposable incomes, weakening demand and triggering downward revisions to baseline GDP growth forecasts.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Confirms the transmission mechanism from energy shocks to growth downgrades — useful for tracking how far current oil-driven inflation could erode consumer spending power before it self-corrects via demand destruction.
insights.locator: In both cases, the cost pressures associated with energy price spikes

themes.theme: Why Oxford Economics' downside scenarios show disinflation, not stagflation

insights.claim: Oxford Economics' downside IFRS 9/CECL scenarios show inflation falling below baseline (not rising further) even during a live war-driven inflation shock, because their statistical scenario methodology is built around demand-side deterioration rather than supply shocks.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: A counterintuitive but methodologically deliberate result: risk managers using these scenarios should not expect downside cases to model further inflation spikes, since the framework treats weaker demand — not additional supply shocks — as the stress driver.
insights.quote: In contrast, our downside IFRS 9 and CECL scenarios see inflation fall below baseline levels, rather than rising further.
insights.locator: In contrast, our downside IFRS 9 and CECL scenarios

insights.claim: Oxford Economics uses forward-looking probability distributions for GDP, unemployment, and house prices to set scenario severity, which satisfies IFRS 9's unbiasedness requirement and lets them attach well-defined probability weights to each scenario.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Explains the structural design choice behind bank/lender provisioning models — useful for credit analysts assessing how peer institutions' IFRS 9/CECL scenario sets are built and whether they are statistically defensible under audit.
insights.locator: We use forward-looking probability distributions for key metrics

insights.claim: The statistical scenario methodology keeps scenarios relatively stable across quarters, limiting excess volatility in clients' loan-loss provisioning.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Stability in provisioning inputs reduces P&L noise for banks — a point relevant to anyone modeling bank earnings volatility tied to credit-loss reserve swings.
insights.locator: Finally, our methodology also succeeds in keeping scenarios

insights.claim: Narrative-based scenario construction applies risk assumptions in a bottom-up, subjective way that lets modelers capture whichever risk theme (e.g., the US/Israel-Iran war) is currently topical, but this subjectivity increases volatility in scenarios and reported P&L over time.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Highlights a key trade-off in credit-risk modeling: narrative scenarios feel more relevant in the moment but inject instability into bank reserves and earnings as the narrative shifts.
insights.locator: This approach differs from a narrative-based approach

insights.claim: Oxford Economics explicitly does not recommend narrative-based scenarios for expected credit loss (ECL) calculation under IFRS 9 or CECL, citing subjectivity, P&L volatility, and an inherent inability to capture unspecified future downside risks.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: A clear, actionable methodological recommendation for banks and auditors choosing between scenario-generation approaches for regulatory credit-loss provisioning.
insights.quote: Reflecting the multiple shortcomings from this approach, we do not recommend the use of narrative-based scenarios for ECL calculation under IFRS 9 or CECL.
insights.locator: Finally, a narrative-based methodology tends to capture only current

insights.claim: In Oxford Economics' statistical downside scenario, weaker aggregate demand and rising unemployment produce lower inflation with no offsetting supply-side shock to commodities or population growth, because adding such a shock would be too subjective a judgment call for the model.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Clarifies the internal logic investors should expect if they see Oxford Economics downside forecasts showing disinflation alongside rising unemployment — it is a deliberate modeling choice, not an oversight.
insights.locator: Under our statistical approach, a downside scenario sees a moderation

insights.claim: Inflation-related downside risk has not been a constant focus even in the recent post-Covid period: before the Iran war, attention had shifted to how quickly rates and inflation would normalize, and pre-pandemic the debate was about how to raise inflation seen as structurally depressed by demographics and excess Chinese supply.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: A reminder that narrative-driven macro focus is highly cyclical and prone to recency bias — investors should be wary of anchoring long-term risk models to whatever inflation narrative is dominant today.
insights.locator: Furthermore, prior to the pandemic, there was much discussion

themes.theme: Statistical scenarios still capture stagflation risk implicitly

insights.claim: Oxford Economics' downside IFRS 9/CECL scenario, modeled at the 85th percentile of its forward-looking distribution, has been persistently more severe on unemployment, house prices, and GDP than the narrative-based stagflationary scenarios in its Global Scenarios Service since 2022.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: A specific, checkable claim (85th percentile threshold) that credit risk teams can use to benchmark severity assumptions against Oxford Economics' own published narrative stagflation cases.
insights.quote: our downside IFRS 9 and CECL scenario, modelled at the 85th percentile of our forward-looking distribution, is persistently more severe than the stagflationary scenarios modelled in our Global Scenarios Service since 2022.
insights.locator: As shown in the accompanying charts, our downside IFRS 9

insights.claim: Although the statistical downside scenarios do not explicitly model stagflation, their severity and representativeness mean they implicitly capture the spillover effects of stagflationary shocks on key credit-loss drivers.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Reassures model users that skipping explicit stagflation narratives does not mean under-provisioning for credit losses in a stagflation scenario.
insights.locator: Hence, although our downside IFRS 9 and CECL scenarios

themes.theme: Interest rates as an imperfect proxy for credit stress

insights.claim: In the downside scenarios, weaker inflation leads to lower policy rates as central banks ease to support activity, which can look counterintuitive in credit loss models since falling rates are usually seen as supportive of borrowers.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Warns model users and analysts that a falling-rate downside scenario is not a bullish signal for credit — it reflects distress-driven easing, not a benign environment.
insights.locator: An exception in this context is the impact on interest rates

insights.claim: Oxford Economics argues policy rates are only a rough proxy for credit conditions, and recommends using more direct measures — write-off rates, insolvencies, and debt burdens — to capture borrower stress more precisely.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: A concrete modeling recommendation: credit risk practitioners should weight direct stress indicators over policy-rate proxies when building or validating provisioning models.
insights.quote: Using more direct measures of credit and borrower stress, such as write-off rates, insolvencies, and debt burdens, overcomes this challenge and ensures a more precise model
insights.locator: Using more direct measures of credit and borrower stress

insights.claim: In the downside IFRS 9/CECL scenarios, write-off rates, insolvencies, and debt burdens all rise despite lower interest rates, because the positive effect of lower rates is more than offset by sharp deterioration in labour markets, incomes, and asset prices.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Confirms that net credit deterioration dominates even with rate relief — a signal that bank loan-loss provisions should be expected to rise in this scenario despite easier monetary policy.
insights.quote: in our downside IFRS 9 and CECL scenarios write-off rates, insolvencies and debt burdens all rise as the positive impact from lower interest rates is more than offset by the sharp deterioration in labour markets, incomes and asset prices.
